title: Sports film

infobox.value: is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") in which any particular [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") plays a prominent role in the film's plot or acts as its central [theme](/wiki/Theme_(arts) "Theme (arts)"). It is a production in which a sport or a sports-related topic is prominently featured or is a focus of the plot. Despite this, sport is ultimately rarely the central concern of such films and sport performs primarily an allegorical role.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Furthermore, [sports fans](/wiki/Fan_(person)#Sports "Fan (person)") are not necessarily the target demographic in such movies, but sports fans tend to maintain a high following and esteem for such movies.

Crosson, Seán (2013). [*Sport and Film*](https://www.academia.edu/27935619). Abingdon, Oxon: Routledge. p. 60. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780415569934](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780415569934 "Special:BookSources/9780415569934").
[*The Lipton Cup: Introducing Sir Thomas Lipton*](https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0003070/), Selig Polyscope Company, 1913-01-20, retrieved 2025-10-02
Firestein, David J. (2007). "Fields of Dreams: American Sports Movies". *E Journal USA*. **12**.

["Sports"](http://web.archive.org/web/20190629014820/https://thescriptlab.com/screenplay/genre/991-sports/). *The Script Lab*. 2011-03-26. Archived from [the original](https://thescriptlab.com/screenplay/genre/991-sports/) on 2019-06-29. Retrieved 2022-11-02.

Williams, Eric R. *Screen adaptation : beyond the basics : techniques for adapting books, comics, and real-life stories into screenplays*. Ayres, Tyler. New York. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1-315-66941-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1-315-66941-0 "Special:BookSources/978-1-315-66941-0"). [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [986993829](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/986993829).

Mangubat, Lio (January 14, 2020). ["The Spin List: These are the sports movies that have won Best Picture at the Oscars"](https://www.spin.ph/life/guide/these-are-the-sports-movies-that-have-won-best-picture-at-the-oscars-a1017-20200114). *The Spin*.

Conti • •, Kristen (27 March 2022). ["Oscar-Worthy Sports Films Throughout History"](https://www.nbcwashington.com/news/sports/oscar-worthy-sports-films-throughout-history/3009912/). *NBC4 Washington*. Retrieved 2022-11-02.

It is a production in which a sport or a sports-related topic is prominently featured or is a focus of the plot. Despite this, sport is ultimately rarely the central concern of such films and sport performs primarily an allegorical role.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Furthermore, [sports fans](/wiki/Fan_(person)#Sports "Fan (person)") are not necessarily the target demographic in such movies, but sports fans tend to maintain a high following and esteem for such movies.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Film genre

A **sports film** is a [film genre](/wiki/Film_genre "Film genre") in which any particular [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") plays a prominent role in the film's plot or acts as its central [theme](/wiki/Theme_(arts) "Theme (arts)"). It is a production in which a sport or a sports-related topic is prominently featured or is a focus of the plot. Despite this, sport is ultimately rarely the central concern of such films and sport performs primarily an allegorical role.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Furthermore, [sports fans](/wiki/Fan_(person)#Sports "Fan (person)") are not necessarily the target demographic in such movies, but sports fans tend to maintain a high following and esteem for such movies.

## Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early history")]

Sources disagree on the first sports film. Short reels depicting unscripted matches were publicly shown as early as 1894, while scripted shorts depicting sports were shown by the end of the decade. The first feature-length sports films would not come until later. Many of the earliest sports films are considered [lost](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film").

The first known sports film was an 1894 reel depicting a [boxing](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") match between Michael Leonard and Jack Cushing, released just four months after the first [movie house](/wiki/Movie_house "Movie house") opened.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) Several other films depicting boxing matches were released in 1894.

Some sources report [*The Ball Game*](/wiki/The_Ball_Game "The Ball Game"), directed by William Heise and produced and distributed by [Thomas Edison's](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") [Edison Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Edison_Manufacturing_Company "Edison Manufacturing Company"), as the first sports film. The film, a short documentary, is a recording of an 1898 game between the [Reading Coal Heavers](/wiki/Reading_Coal_Heavers "Reading Coal Heavers") and the [Newark Bears](/wiki/Newark_Bears "Newark Bears"). Edison produced a short film based on the poem "[Casey at the Bat](/wiki/Casey_at_the_Bat "Casey at the Bat")" the following year.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

Sources differ as to the first feature-length sports film. 1913's *The Lipton Cup* is a feature-length drama centered on sailing.[[3]](#cite_note-3) 1915's [*Little Sunset*](/wiki/Little_Sunset "Little Sunset") was the first feature-length film depicting baseball.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The first feature-length [biographical film](/wiki/Biographical_film "Biographical film") depicting a sport was *Right Off the Bat,* released several months after *Little Sunset* (some sources claim that *Right Off the Bat* was the first feature-length sports film).[[5]](#cite_note-5) The film, directed by [Hugh Reticker](/wiki/Hugh_Reticker "Hugh Reticker"), depicts the life of player [Mike Donlin](/wiki/Mike_Donlin "Mike Donlin"). Donlin appears as himself in the film.

Despite their early popularity, sports films earned a reputation for frequent [box-office bombs](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb") and struggled to earn the support of large production companies after their popularity in the 1930s.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Subgenres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Subgenres")]

Several sub-categories of sports films can be identified, although the delineations between these subgenres, much as in live action, are somewhat fluid.

The most common sports subgenres depicted in movies are sports drama and sports comedy. Both categories typically employ playground settings, match, game creatures and other elements commonly associated with biological stories.

Sports films tend to feature a more richly developed sport world, and may also be more player-oriented or thematically complex. Often, they feature a hero of adventure origins and a clear distinction between loss and victory set against each other in a play time struggle.

Thematically, the story is often one of "our team" versus "their team"; their team will always try to win, and our team will show the world that they deserve recognition or redemption; the story does not always have to involve a team. The story could also be about an individual athlete or the story could focus on an individual playing on a team.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Sports comedy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sports comedy")]

Sports comedy combines the sports film genre with [comedy film](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film") elements. Traditionally, these films heavily rely on slapstick humor and very physical comedy, such as someone getting hurt in a comical way. A typical storyline may revolve around someone losing sight of the sport they are playing and trying to get back into it.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Examples and staples of the genre include *[The Waterboy](/wiki/The_Waterboy "The Waterboy")*, [*The Longest Yard*](/wiki/The_Longest_Yard_(2005_film) "The Longest Yard (2005 film)"), *[Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby](/wiki/Talladega_Nights:_The_Ballad_of_Ricky_Bobby "Talladega Nights: The Ballad of Ricky Bobby")*, and *[Blades of Glory](/wiki/Blades_of_Glory "Blades of Glory")*.[[8]](#cite_note-:1-8)

### Sports drama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sports drama")]

See also: [Drama (film and television)](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)")

Sports drama combines the sports film genre with drama film elements. These films rely on conflict, usually revolving around an athlete or a team. These dramas can further be broken up into categories, some movies focusing on race such as *[42](/wiki/42_(film) "42 (film)")* (2013), or focusing on a specific moment in history like *[I, Tonya](/wiki/I,_Tonya "I, Tonya")* (2018).[[9]](#cite_note-9) Examples of this overall genre/type include: *[Body and Soul](/wiki/Body_and_Soul_(1947_film) "Body and Soul (1947 film)")* (1947), *[The Hustler](/wiki/The_Hustler "The Hustler")* (1961), *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")* (1976), *[Hoosiers](/wiki/Hoosiers_(film) "Hoosiers (film)")* (1986), *[Remember the Titans](/wiki/Remember_the_Titans "Remember the Titans")* (2000), [Lagaan](/wiki/Lagaan "Lagaan") (2001), *[Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball_(film) "Moneyball (film)")* (2011), *[Ford v Ferrari](/wiki/Ford_v_Ferrari "Ford v Ferrari")* (2019), *[Ferrari](/wiki/Ferrari_(2023_film) "Ferrari (2023 film)")* (2023) and the *[Goal!](/wiki/Goal!_(film_series) "Goal! (film series)")* trilogy.

## Award-winning films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Award-winning films")]

There have been numerous sports movies that have become award winning phenomena. Several films have been nominated for and won the highest award of [Best Picture](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Picture "Academy Award for Best Picture") at the [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), including *[Chariots of Fire](/wiki/Chariots_of_Fire "Chariots of Fire")* (1981), *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")* (1976), and *[Million Dollar Baby](/wiki/Million_Dollar_Baby "Million Dollar Baby")* (2004).[[10]](#cite_note-10) Other movies that received awards of a high caliber are *[Jerry Maguire](/wiki/Jerry_Maguire "Jerry Maguire")* (Best Supporting Actor, 1996), *[Bull Durham](/wiki/Bull_Durham "Bull Durham")* (Best Original Screenplay, 1988), and *[The Karate Kid](/wiki/The_Karate_Kid "The Karate Kid")* (Best Supporting Actor, 1984).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## List of sports films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sports_film&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: List of sports films")]

Main article: [List of sports films](/wiki/List_of_sports_films "List of sports films")
